sources.title: Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program: How Penalties Work

output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program established by the Affordable Care Act that began reducing payments to hospitals in fiscal year 2013. The program applies to general short-term acute care hospitals paid under Medicare's Inpatient Prospective Payment System and excludes critical access hospitals long-term care hospitals rehabilitation facilities psychiatric hospitals and children's hospitals.

Penalties are calculated using an Excess Readmission Ratio (ERR) that compares a hospital's predicted readmission rate to the expected rate for similar patients at an average hospital. Hospitals with ERRs above 1.0 face payment reductions of up to 3% of their total Medicare base operating payments while those at or below 1.0 receive no penalty. The program tracks six specific conditions: acute myocardial infarction chronic obstructive pulmonary disease heart failure pneumonia coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective primary total hip or knee replacement.

For fiscal year 2026 approximately 78% of eligible hospitals face some level of penalty with about 240 hospitals losing at least 1% of their Medicare payments. The performance period for FY 2026 penalties covers discharges from July 1 2021 through June 30 2024 representing a three-year data window. Beginning with FY 2027 CMS will shorten this measurement window to two years making recent performance carry more weight in penalty calculations.

The program includes risk adjustment for patient complexity and peer group stratification to account for hospitals serving different populations particularly safety-net hospitals with higher proportions of low-income patients. Planned readmissions are excluded from calculations using CMS's Planned Readmission Algorithm and the 30-day readmission window captures unplanned returns to any acute care hospital regardless of the principal diagnosis.

Penalties of up to 3% of total Medicare base operating payments are assessed using an Excess Readmission Ratio comparing a hospital's readmission rate to an expected benchmark. For fiscal year 2026 approximately 78% of eligible hospitals face some penalty with about 240 hospitals losing at least 1% of Medicare payments.

sources.title: Hospitals with the Highest Readmission Penalties in the U.S.

output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program that began in fiscal year 2013 to reduce payments to hospitals with excess readmissions. The program penalizes hospitals for excessive 30-day readmissions in six specific conditions: acute myocardial infarction chronic obstructive pulmonary disease heart failure pneumonia coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective primary total hip or knee arthroplasty.

Penalties are calculated based on a hospital's excess readmission ratio compared to national averages with payment reductions capped at 3 percent of Medicare fee-for-service base operating diagnosis-related group payments. Hospitals with readmission ratios of one or less receive no penalties while those with higher ratios face increasing penalty rates up to the maximum 3 percent reduction.

The program applies payment reductions to all Medicare fee-for-service payments during the fiscal year from October 1 to September 30. CMS sends confidential Hospital-Specific Reports annually and provides hospitals 30 days to review their data and request corrections before finalizing penalties.

Beginning in fiscal year 2019 CMS implemented peer grouping methodology to assess hospital performance relative to other hospitals with similar proportions of dual-eligible Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries. This change was mandated by the 21st Century Cures Act to maintain budget neutrality while accounting for socioeconomic factors affecting readmission rates.

Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent of Medicare fee-for-service base operating DRG payments, applied across all Medicare FFS payments from October 1 to September 30. Hospitals with excess readmission ratios at or below 1.0 face no penalty. Starting in FY2019, the 21st Century Cures Act mandated peer grouping by proportion of dual-eligible beneficiaries to account for socioeconomic factors.

sources.title: CMS Readmission Rates: How They're Calculated and Penalized

output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program that reduces payments to hospitals with higher-than-expected 30-day unplanned readmission rates for six specific conditions: acute myocardial infarction heart failure pneumonia chronic obstructive pulmonary disease elective primary total hip/knee arthroplasty and coronary artery bypass graft surgery. Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent and apply to all Medicare fee-for-service base operating diagnosis-related group payments during the entire fiscal year from October 1 to September 30.

CMS calculates penalties using the Excess Readmission Ratio which compares each hospital's risk-adjusted actual readmissions to expected readmissions based on patient characteristics like age sex and clinical conditions. Since fiscal year 2019 hospitals are grouped into five peer groups based on the proportion of patients who are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid with each hospital's performance compared against the median of its peer group rather than a universal benchmark.

For fiscal year 2025 approximately 2400 hospitals face some level of penalty with about 7 percent facing reductions of 1 percent or more and 71.6 percent receiving penalties of less than 1 percent. The program uses a three-year rolling performance period to calculate penalties with fiscal year 2025 penalties based on claims data from July 2020 through June 2023.

Hospitals receive confidential Hospital-Specific Reports annually and have 30 days to review their data submit questions about calculations and request corrections before CMS finalizes the penalty determinations. The program maintains budget neutrality through a neutrality modifier that ensures total Medicare savings under the peer group system match what the previous non-stratified method would have produced.

Since FY2019 hospitals are grouped into five peer groups based on dual Medicare-Medicaid eligibility share and compared against their peer group median rather than a universal benchmark. sources.title: Hospitals with the Highest Readmission Penalties in the U.S.

output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program established by Section 1886(q) of the Social Security Act that began October 1 2012. The program reduces payments to hospitals with excess readmissions compared to national averages for specific conditions including acute myocardial infarction chronic obstructive pulmonary disease heart failure pneumonia coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective primary total hip or knee arthroplasty.

Penalties are calculated using the excess readmission ratio which measures a hospital's relative performance as a ratio of predicted-to-expected unplanned readmissions rates. Hospitals can receive payment reductions ranging from 1% to 3% of their Medicare reimbursements with the maximum penalty being 3% applied to all discharges regardless of condition.

The program measures unplanned readmissions that occur within 30 days of discharge from the initial admission to the same hospital or another applicable acute care hospital. The payment adjustment factor is calculated as a weighted average of a hospital's performance across all readmission measures during the performance period and applies to all discharges in the applicable fiscal year.

For fiscal year 2024 nine hospitals received the maximum 3% penalty with HCA Florida St. Lucie Hospital being the largest penalized facility based on 13779 annual discharges. The program aims to incentivize hospitals to improve communication and care coordination to better engage patients and caregivers in discharge planning and reduce avoidable readmissions.

It penalizes hospitals with excess 30-day unplanned readmissions for six specific conditions including acute myocardial infarction heart failure pneumonia COPD CABG surgery and elective hip or knee arthroplasty. Penalties range from 1% to 3% of all Medicare reimbursements based on each hospital's excess readmission ratio. In fiscal year 2024 nine hospitals received the maximum 3% penalty with HCA Florida St. Lucie Hospital being the largest penalized facility at 13779 annual discharges.

sources.title: CMS Readmission Rates: How They're Calculated and Penalized

output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP) is a Medicare value-based purchasing program that reduces payments to hospitals with excess 30-day unplanned readmissions beginning October 1 2012. The program measures readmissions for six specific conditions: acute myocardial infarction heart failure pneumonia chronic obstructive pulmonary disease coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective primary total hip or knee arthroplasty.

Penalties are calculated using the Excess Readmission Ratio (ERR) which compares a hospital's risk-adjusted actual readmissions to expected readmissions for each measured condition. Since fiscal year 2019 hospitals are grouped into five peer groups based on the proportion of patients dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid with performance compared against the median ERR of hospitals in the same peer group rather than a fixed benchmark.

Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent and apply to all Medicare fee-for-service base operating diagnosis-related group payments during the entire fiscal year from October 1 to September 30. The penalty calculation uses a three-year rolling performance period and approximately 2400 hospitals face some level of penalty with about 8 percent facing reductions of 1 percent or more in recent years.

A readmission counts as any unplanned return to any acute care hospital within 30 days of discharge regardless of whether the patient returns to the same hospital or is readmitted for the same diagnosis. Scheduled returns for planned procedures are excluded and hospitals need at least 25 eligible discharges for a condition during the performance period for it to be included in penalty calculations.

metadata.question: Create exactly 4 bullets, each a single sentence with zero commas

output.summary: The CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP) launched October 1 2012 and reduces Medicare payments to hospitals with excess 30-day unplanned readmissions across six tracked conditions: acute myocardial infarction; heart failure; pneumonia; COPD; CABG surgery; and elective hip or knee arthroplasty. Penalties are derived from the Excess Readmission Ratio comparing risk-adjusted actual readmissions to expected readmissions and since fiscal year 2019 hospitals are benchmarked against peer groups stratified by dual Medicare-Medicaid eligibility rates. Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent and apply to all Medicare fee-for-service base operating DRG payments across the full fiscal year using a three-year rolling performance window. Approximately 2400 hospitals face some penalty annually with roughly 8 percent incurring reductions of 1 percent or more.

Minimum of 25 eligible discharges required per condition for inclusion in penalty calculations

metadata.label: r1
metadata.question: CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program penalties details

output.question: Research the CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program penalties. Summarize in exactly 4 bullets. Each bullet must be a single sentence containing NO commas. The critic must verify zero commas appear in any bullet.
output.findings:
The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program that reduces payments to hospitals with excess readmissions beginning in fiscal year 2013. The program applies to subsection (d) hospitals and covers six specific conditions and procedures including acute myocardial infarction chronic obstructive pulmonary disease heart failure pneumonia coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective primary total hip or knee arthroplasty.

Payment reductions are calculated using a payment adjustment factor based on the hospital's excess readmission ratio which measures predicted-to-expected unplanned readmission rates within 30 days of discharge. The payment reduction is capped at 3 percent meaning the maximum payment adjustment factor is 0.97. These reductions apply to all Medicare fee-for-service base operating diagnosis-related group payments during the applicable fiscal year regardless of the specific condition.

The program uses a peer grouping methodology beginning in fiscal year 2019 that assesses hospital performance relative to other hospitals with similar proportions of dual-eligible Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries. CMS provides hospitals with confidential Hospital-Specific Reports annually and allows a 30-day Review and Correction period for hospitals to review their data and request calculation corrections before penalties are applied.

fields.bullet_3: Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent meaning the maximum payment adjustment factor applied to Medicare fee-for-service base operating DRG payments is 0.97
fields.bullet_4: Since fiscal year 2019 CMS has used a peer grouping methodology that compares hospitals only against peers with similar proportions of dual-eligible Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries

output.final_answer:
• The Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program is a Medicare value-based purchasing program that reduces payments to hospitals with excess readmissions starting in fiscal year 2013
• The program covers six conditions including acute myocardial infarction and heart failure and pneumonia and COPD and coronary artery bypass graft surgery and elective total hip or knee arthroplasty
• Payment reductions are capped at 3 percent meaning the maximum payment adjustment factor applied to Medicare fee-for-service base operating DRG payments is 0.97
• Since fiscal year 2019 CMS has used a peer grouping methodology that compares hospitals only against peers with similar proportions of dual-eligible Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries
